title: When Your Doctor Dismisses Your Pain
dek: *When a doctor waves away your pain as stress or anxiety or "just part of getting older," something happens in your body that goes beyond frustration. The dismissal itself becomes a stressor, layering new tension over the original hurt. Here's how to navigate medical gaslighting while staying connected to what you know is true.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Medical dismissal of pain creates both physiological and psychological harm that compounds the original symptom. Your body remembers every instance of being ignored, and that memory shapes how pain signals travel through your nervous system. Learning to advocate for yourself while simultaneously befriending your own sensations offers a path through this difficult territory.

Have you been rehearsing what you'll say at your next appointment, editing your symptoms down to make them sound more believable? That internal editing, that self-censorship before you even open your mouth in the exam room, tells you something important. You've already absorbed the message that your pain might not be real enough, significant enough, or legitimate enough to warrant attention. And that absorption happens in the body, not just the mind. ## The Physiology of Being Dismissed When someone in a position of authority tells you that what you're experiencing isn't real or isn't important, your nervous system faces a paradox. You have direct sensory evidence, your own lived experience of pain or discomfort. But an external authority figure is contradicting that evidence. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body shows us that this kind of contradiction, this severing between inner experience and external validation, creates its own form of dysregulation in the autonomic nervous system. Your body knows what it feels. When that knowing is dismissed, you don't simply forget the pain. Instead, you often develop a split: part of you that feels, and part of you that monitors and judges whether that feeling is acceptable. This internal division requires energy. It creates tension. In my own practice, I've watched people develop chronic neck and shoulder pain that correlates directly with the effort of holding this split in place. The dismissal also triggers a stress response. Your cortisol levels rise. Your breathing becomes shallower. The original pain now has company: the physiological cascade of not being believed. According to the CDC's guidance on stress and the body, chronic stress affects every system, from digestion to immune function to pain perception itself. ## What Medical Gaslighting Actually Is Medical gaslighting isn't simply a difference of opinion between you and your doctor. It's a pattern of dismissal that makes you question your own perception. The doctor might attribute physical symptoms to anxiety without running appropriate tests. They might tell you the pain is normal when it clearly interferes with your daily life. They might suggest you're exaggerating, being dramatic, or seeking attention. Women, people of color, fat people, and those with invisible illnesses experience this dismissal at significantly higher rates. The bias isn't always conscious, but it's measurable in outcomes: longer times to diagnosis, fewer referrals to specialists, less aggressive pain management. The gaslighting becomes internalized. You start to wonder if you're making too much of things. You minimize your symptoms when talking to friends. You push through pain that deserves attention. You become your own dismissive doctor, and that internal voice is often harsher than any external one. ## The Anger That Has Nowhere to Go There's a specific kind of rage that comes from not being believed about your own body. It's clean anger, justified anger, the kind that wants to protect you. But in the power dynamic of a medical appointment, that anger often has no safe outlet. Robert Masters writes about the importance of distinguishing between different qualities of anger, and the anger of being dismissed about pain sits in a particular category. It's not the anger of a boundary violation you can simply walk away from. You need medical care. You're dependent on this system that's failing you. So the anger turns inward, becomes depression, or gets expressed sideways in irritability that confuses the people around you. Over the years I've seen how unexpressed anger about medical dismissal lodges in the jaw, the throat, the solar plexus. It creates its own pain signature. For some support in working with intense emotions in the body, [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) offers practices for channeling rather than suppressing what wants to move. Your anger is information. It's telling you that something unjust is happening. The challenge is finding ways to honor that information without letting it consume you or without turning it against yourself. ## Your Body Keeps the Score of Every Dismissal Each time your pain is waved away, your nervous system logs the experience. Pat Ogden's work in sensorimotor psychotherapy emphasizes how the body holds procedural memory, memory that lives in gesture and posture and automatic response rather than in narrative. You might not consciously remember every dismissive comment from every doctor, but your body remembers the feeling of bracing for disbelief. It remembers the sensation of making yourself smaller, less demanding. It remembers the collapse that happens when you leave an appointment without help. These body memories affect how you show up in future medical encounters. You might rush through your symptom list, apologizing for taking up time. You might present with a flat affect that actually makes you seem less credible. You might dissociate slightly, disconnecting from the very sensations you came to discuss. Notice what happens in your body right now as you read this. Is there tension in your chest? A tightness in your throat? That's not random. That's your system responding to the recognition of a pattern it knows intimately. ## Building Your Own Body Literacy One response to medical dismissal is to become fluent in your own body's language. Not to replace medical care, but to stand more firmly in what you know when you seek it. This means developing a practice of checking in with your body outside of crisis moments. What does your baseline feel like? Where do you hold tension when you're calm? How does your digestion respond to different foods? What's your relationship with your menstrual cycle, if you have one? Keeping a symptom journal helps, but make it sensory rather than just diagnostic. Instead of writing "headache," try "tight band around forehead, pressure behind right eye, nausea that starts in my upper stomach and rises." This kind of specific, body-based language gives you concrete details to share with providers. It also trains you to stay present with sensation rather than immediately jumping to interpretation or catastrophizing. Some people find herbs useful for this kind of body literacy work. [Banyan Botanicals Organic Shatavari Tablets - 90 Tablets](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000703AQ4?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support hormonal awareness in bodies with menstrual cycles, making it easier to track patterns. The point isn't to self-diagnose, but to gather data from your own experience. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Grounding Before Medical Appointments Try this before your next doctor's visit. You can do it in your car or in the waiting room. Sit with your feet flat on the floor. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Take three slow breaths, noticing which hand moves more. Most people are chest breathers when anxious, but deeper belly breathing signals safety to your nervous system. Now, say out loud (even in a whisper): "I know what I feel. My pain is real. I deserve care." Say it three times. Notice any resistance that comes up. Notice if your body wants to qualify the statement or make it smaller. Finally, imagine roots growing from your feet into the floor. You're not trying to be calm or nice or accommodating. You're just trying to be present and grounded in your own experience. That's enough. ## Finding Providers Who Actually Listen Not all doctors dismiss pain. The challenge is finding the ones who don't before you're too exhausted from being dismissed to keep looking. Word of mouth matters here. Ask friends, especially friends with similar bodies or similar conditions, who they see. Online communities for specific diagnoses often maintain lists of recommended providers. Some medical practices explicitly state their commitment to trauma-informed care or to serving marginalized communities. In a first appointment, you're also interviewing the doctor. Do they make eye contact? Do they ask follow-up questions about your symptoms? Do they acknowledge when they don't know something? Do they respect your expertise about your own body? If you can, bring someone with you to appointments. Studies show that patients with advocates get taken more seriously. It's infuriating that this is true, but it is true. Use what works. You might also explore integrative approaches that complement conventional medicine. Many people find that practitioners who work somatically, who understand the bodymind as one system, offer a different quality of listening. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return often to the value of multiple perspectives on healing. ## The Nervous System Needs Tending Too While you're navigating the medical system, your nervous system needs support. The stress of being dismissed, of chronic pain, of fighting to be believed, all of this taxes your resources. [Host Defense Stamets 7 (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002WIVHNM?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers immune support that can be particularly helpful when stress is high. Some people find that regular self-massage with oils helps maintain a sense of body ownership and care. [Banyan Botanicals Brahmi Oil (Sesame Base)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D79SNVBL?tag=spankyspinola-20) has a calming quality that makes it useful for evening foot or scalp massage. Simple practices matter: warm baths, time in nature, moving your body in ways that feel good rather than punitive. These aren't substitutes for medical care, but they're part of maintaining yourself while you search for appropriate care. The nervous system also needs what Peter Levine calls "pendulation," the ability to move between activation and rest. When you're in chronic pain or chronic medical gaslighting, you can get stuck in activation. Finding moments of genuine ease, even brief ones, helps restore some flexibility to your system. ## When the Dismissal Comes From Inside Sometimes the harshest dismissal of your pain comes from your own mind. You've internalized all those messages about being dramatic, being weak, being too sensitive. You've learned to minimize and push through and tough it out. This internal dismissal is often more damaging than external dismissal because it's constant. It doesn't end when the appointment ends. It follows you home. It wakes you up at night. Working with this internal critic requires gentleness, which might sound paradoxical. You can't bully yourself into self-compassion. What you can do is start to notice when that dismissive voice speaks up. What does it sound like? Whose voice is it really? What is it trying to protect you from? Often, the internal dismissal is trying to protect you from disappointment or from being hurt again by external dismissal. If you dismiss your own pain first, maybe it won't hurt as much when someone else does. But this protection costs you your relationship with your own experience. Robert Masters talks about the importance of differentiating between parts of ourselves, meeting each part with curiosity rather than judgment. The part that dismisses your pain probably learned that strategy somewhere. It might have been useful once. You can acknowledge that while also choosing something different now. ## The Political Dimension of Pain Your individual experience of pain and dismissal sits within larger systems of power and oppression. Medical training has historically centered white male bodies as the default. Pain in other bodies gets interpreted through that lens, often incorrectly. Women's pain is more likely to be attributed to emotional or psychological causes. Black patients receive less pain medication than white patients for the same conditions. Fat patients are told to lose weight before their symptoms are investigated. Trans and nonbinary people often avoid medical care entirely because of the dismissal and discrimination they anticipate. Understanding this isn't about adding to your sense of powerlessness. It's about recognizing that the problem isn't you. You're not too sensitive. You're not making it up. You're navigating a system with real, documented biases. This recognition can actually be freeing. It allows you to stop trying to be the perfect patient who finally says things in just the right way to be believed. It allows you to see the problem clearly and to connect with others who face similar challenges. There's power in that collective recognition. For more on how systemic issues intersect with individual healing, [the about page](/about) outlines some of the frameworks we work within. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I know if I'm actually being dismissed or if the doctor just has a different opinion? A difference of opinion includes explanation, discussion of alternatives, and acknowledgment of your experience. Dismissal sounds like "it's all in your head," "you just need to relax," or "that's normal for someone your age" without further investigation. If you leave the appointment feeling unheard or if your symptoms weren't adequately addressed, trust that feeling. ### Should I bring research articles to my appointment to prove my symptoms are real? This is a personal choice. Some people find that bringing peer-reviewed research helps, especially for rare or poorly understood conditions. Others find that it creates defensiveness in the provider. If you do bring research, frame it as collaboration rather than confrontation: "I found this article and wondered if it might be relevant to what I'm experiencing." ### What if I can't afford to keep changing doctors until I find one who listens? This is a real constraint. If you're limited in options, focus on documentation. Keep detailed records of your symptoms, when they occur, what makes them better or worse. Request that dismissive comments be added to your chart. Ask for referrals to specialists even if the doctor seems skeptical. Create a paper trail that future providers can see, showing a pattern of symptoms that weren't adequately addressed. ## What Your Body Already Knows Your pain is not a character flaw. It's not evidence of weakness or proof that you're difficult. It's information, a signal from your body that something needs attention. That signal doesn't disappear just because someone in a white coat fails to hear it. The work isn't to convince yourself that your pain is real. Your body already knows it's real. The work is to maintain that knowing in the face of dismissal, to keep the channel open between your sensation and your awareness. This is harder than it sounds. It requires a kind of stubborn tenderness toward yourself, a willingness to keep showing up for your own experience even when others don't. Some days this feels impossible. Some days the weight of not being believed crushes your certainty about what you feel. That's when you need to borrow belief from somewhere else, maybe from [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) or from a friend who knows your truth or from the simple fact that pain doesn't require an audience to exist. It exists because it exists. Your work is just to stay present with it, to let it teach you what it needs to teach you, and to keep searching for the care you deserve. The search itself is an act of self-respect, a way of saying that your comfort and your healing matter, whether or not the first doctor you see recognizes that truth. ## Keep reading - [the assessments hub](/assessments) - [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) - [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on when your doctor dismisses your pain, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
